title: Build an Automation (Smart Flow)
lede: Connect a trigger to a chain of actions in iMash's visual Automation builder — so new leads get follow-up calls, records update themselves, and busywork simply stops being your job. No code required.

steps.title: Open the Automation Dashboard
steps.content:
Every automation you build lives in one visual workspace. Let's open it.

  1. In the left sidebar, under Intelligence Center, click Automation. (You can also click the Automation tab at the top of the app, then Automation Flows.)
  2. The Flows dashboard opens. Here's what you'll see:
    • Flows / Runs / Issues tabs — your flow list, every execution, and any failures that need attention.
    • Folders panel — organize flows into folders; All flows and Uncategorized are built in, and the + button adds new folders.
    • Flow name, Status and Connection filters — quickly find a flow in a long list.
    • A left rail with Flows, Custom Tables, System Tables, MCP, Todos and Connections — this guide focuses on Flows; Connections is where linked app credentials live.

images.alt: Automation dashboard showing the Flows list
images.caption: The Automation dashboard — every flow, run and issue in one place.

images.alt: Automation dashboard showing the Flows list
images.caption: The Automation dashboard — every flow, run and issue in one place.

steps.title: Create a New Flow
steps.content:
1. Click Create flow in the top-right corner.
  2. Pick how you want to start:
    • From scratch — a blank canvas (what we'll use in this guide).
    • Use a template — browse ready-made flows and customize them.
    • From local file — import a flow that was previously exported as a JSON file (great for copying flows between accounts).
  3. Click From scratch. A new flow named Untitled opens in the builder.

images.alt: Create flow menu with From scratch, Use a template, and From local file options
images.caption: Create flow → From scratch starts a blank automation.

images.alt: Create flow menu with From scratch, Use a template, and From local file options
images.caption: Create flow → From scratch starts a blank automation.

steps.title: Choose Your Trigger
steps.content:
Every flow starts with exactly one trigger — the event that kicks everything off. Until you pick one, the canvas shows 1. Select Trigger with an "Empty Trigger" warning and a Complete 1 step badge.

  1. Click the 1. Select Trigger block (the picker usually opens by itself on a new flow).
  2. Use the Search box, or narrow the list with the All / AI / Core / Apps filter pills.
  3. Popular trigger sources include Webhook (receive data from any external system), Schedule (run on a timer), plus apps like Gmail, Google Sheets, Slack and Human Input.
  4. Click a piece on the left to preview its available triggers on the right, then click a trigger to select it.

images.alt: Trigger picker with search box, filter pills and popular pieces
images.caption: Click the Select Trigger block and browse the trigger catalog.

images.alt: Trigger picker with search box, filter pills and popular pieces
images.caption: Click the Select Trigger block and browse the trigger catalog.

steps.title: Example: Run on a Schedule
steps.content:
Want a flow that runs on autopilot with no external event at all? Pick the Schedule piece and choose how often it fires:

  • Every X Minutes — perfect for frequent checks (e.g. poll for new records).
  • Every Hour, Every Day, Every Week, Every Month — classic recurring jobs like daily digests or weekly reports.
  • Cron Expression — full control for advanced timing patterns.
💡 Tip: If another system should push data into iMash the moment something happens (a form fill, a new order, a lead from an ad platform), choose Webhook → Catch Webhook instead — that's what we'll use next.

images.alt: Schedule piece showing Every X Minutes, Every Hour, Every Day, Every Week, Every Month and Cron Expression triggers
images.caption: The Schedule piece offers timer triggers from minutes to a full cron expression.

images.alt: Schedule piece showing Every X Minutes, Every Hour, Every Day, Every Week, Every Month and Cron Expression triggers
images.caption: The Schedule piece offers timer triggers from minutes to a full cron expression.

steps.title: Configure the Trigger (Catch Webhook)
steps.content:
Once you select Webhook → Catch Webhook, the trigger's settings panel opens on the right — and your flow instantly gets its own private URL:

  • Live URL — copy this with the copy icon and paste it into the external system that should trigger the flow. Any HTTP request sent to it starts your published flow.
  • Test URL — a separate URL for safely testing the current draft without touching the live version.
  • Synchronous Requests — add /sync to the end of the URL if the caller expects a response back (pair it with a Return Response action later in the flow).
  • Generate Sample Data → Test Trigger — send a real request to the Test URL, then click this so the builder captures the incoming data. That sample is what lets later steps map fields like a phone number or name.
⚠️ Don't skip Test Trigger. Without sample data, later steps can't show you the incoming fields to map, and testing the whole flow is blocked.

images.alt: Builder canvas with the Catch Webhook trigger and its settings panel showing the Live URL
images.caption: Catch Webhook gives your flow a unique URL any system can call.

images.alt: Builder canvas with the Catch Webhook trigger and its settings panel showing the Live URL
images.caption: Catch Webhook gives your flow a unique URL any system can call.

steps.title: Add an Action
steps.content:
Actions are the steps that actually do the work. They run top to bottom, and you can chain as many as you need.

  1. Hover over the line below your trigger and click the + button.
  2. The action picker opens — the same search box and All / AI / Core / Apps filters as before. Popular picks include HTTP (call any API), OpenAI, Gmail, Google Sheets and Human Input.
  3. Under Core you'll also find flow-control pieces like branches and loops for smarter logic.
  4. Click a piece, then click the specific action you want — it's added to the canvas as step 2.

images.alt: Action picker opened from the plus button below the trigger
images.caption: Click + below the trigger to add your first action.

images.alt: Action picker opened from the plus button below the trigger
images.caption: Click + below the trigger to add your first action.

steps.title: Use the iMash Actions (CRM Data + AI Calls)
steps.content:
Here's where automations get superpowers: the built-in iMash piece connects flows directly to your CRM and your AI call center.

  1. In the action picker's search box, type imash.
  2. The iMash piece appears with five actions:
    • Find System Table Rows — look up existing records (leads, contacts, jobs and more).
    • Create System Table Record(s) — add new records, e.g. create a lead from webhook data.
    • Update System Table Row — change an existing record's fields.
    • Delete System Table Row(s) — remove records.
    • Create Outbound Call — have one of your AI agents place a phone call. New lead in → AI follow-up call out, in seconds.
  3. After adding an action, fill in its fields in the right-hand panel. Click into any field to open the Data Selector and insert values from earlier steps — for example the phone number that arrived in your webhook.
💡 The killer combo: Catch Webhook → Create System Table Record(s) (save the lead) → Create Outbound Call (AI calls them back immediately). Speed-to-lead, fully automated.

images.alt: Action picker searching imash, showing System Table actions and Create Outbound Call
images.caption: Search "imash" to work with CRM records or launch AI calls from a flow.

images.alt: Action picker searching imash, showing System Table actions and Create Outbound Call
images.caption: Search "imash" to work with CRM records or launch AI calls from a flow.

steps.title: Test, Then Publish
steps.content:
Time to bring it to life.

  1. Click Test Flow at the top of the canvas. The builder runs every step with your sample trigger data and shows each step's input and output — fix any step that reports an error.
  2. You can also test a single step from its settings panel before running the whole flow.
  3. When everything passes, click Publish in the top-right corner. Your flow is now live: it runs automatically every time the trigger fires.
  4. Need to pause it later? Use the flow's status toggle in the Flows list to turn it off without deleting anything.
⚠️ Publish is required. A draft flow never runs on its own — external systems calling the Live URL do nothing until the flow is published.

steps.title: Rename, Duplicate, Export and More
steps.content:
Give your flow a name you'll recognize in six months — "Untitled" won't cut it.

  1. In the builder, click the ˅ arrow next to the flow name at the top-left.
  2. The flow menu offers:
    • Rename — type the new name and press Enter.
    • Move To — file the flow into a folder.
    • Duplicate — copy a working flow as the starting point for a new one.
    • Import / Export — bring a flow in from a JSON file, or save this one out (backups, sharing, moving between accounts).
    • Delete — remove the flow permanently.
  3. Also in the top bar: Versions shows the flow's version history, and Runs jumps to this flow's execution log.

images.alt: Flow name menu with Rename, Move To, Duplicate, Import, Export and Delete
images.caption: The flow menu — rename, organize, duplicate, import/export or delete.

images.alt: Flow name menu with Rename, Move To, Duplicate, Import, Export and Delete
images.caption: The flow menu — rename, organize, duplicate, import/export or delete.

steps.title: Monitor Runs and Issues
steps.content:
Once flows are live, the dashboard tells you exactly how they're performing.

  1. Back on the Automation dashboard, click the Runs tab to see every execution with its Flow, Status, Start Time and Duration.
  2. Filter by Flow name, Status, or Pick a date range to zero in on what you're looking for.
  3. Click any run to replay it step by step and inspect each step's exact input and output — the fastest way to debug.
  4. Select failed runs and click Retry to run them again.
  5. The Issues tab collects flows that are failing repeatedly, so problems never slip by unnoticed.

That's it — you've built, tested and published your first Smart Flow. Every one you add from here compounds the time you save. 🚀

images.alt: Runs tab with Flow name, Status and date range filters and the Retry button
images.caption: The Runs tab logs every execution — filter, inspect and retry from here.

images.alt: Runs tab with Flow name, Status and date range filters and the Retry button
images.caption: The Runs tab logs every execution — filter, inspect and retry from here.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaTitle: Build an Automation (Smart Flow) | iMash Documentation
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to iMash Smart Flows: create a flow, pick a webhook or schedule trigger, add iMash actions like Create Outbound Call, then test, publish and monitor runs.
